story:
It starts with a radio squawk — “Code 6 in Housewares” — and ends with me hungry, filling out paperwork about towels people are now treating like holy artifacts. My partner and I roll up to SuperMart. Dispatch keeps repeating “Code 6” like it’s a password. No one on scene — not staff, not management, not even the employee who says she made the page — can define it. Still counts as my lunch break dying for the cause.

Definitions fly. Employee #1, nails still drying, swears Code 6 means “vomit cleanup.” Store Manager claims it’s a “shoplifter in progress,” though the only offender is a five-year-old free-sampling grapes like it’s a tasting menu. Union Rep pops out of Housewares to announce Code 6 is corporate for “layoffs,” demands my badge number, and somehow turns a mystery page into a labor dispute. A customer shouts it means “active shooter,” and that’s when the store tips into slapstick: half the crowd hides behind bath towels, some crawl across tile, and one guy charges around brandishing a Swiffer like a rifle. In the chaos, a panicked shopper with a lawfully carried concealed handgun accidentally discharges two rounds. No one is injured. The shots hit a stack of towels, which — per eyewitness faith — “absorb” them. Later, shoppers refuse to surrender the “miracle towels.” I do not endorse magical textiles; I do endorse basic firearm safety.

EMTs arrive, declaring “Code 6” means sirens-only transport. Considering there isn’t a drop of blood, they pivot to a heated negotiation over the last bag of Cool Ranch Doritos. In my training, Code 6 meant free station coffee. That came closest to being useful and was, tragically, not present.

Housewares is otherwise stable: a crooked candle display and a clerk crying into discount pillows. Said clerk reports that the candles rearranged themselves to spell “CODE 6.” No photos, only tears. Witness statements are a flock of pigeons: loud, messy, contradictory. Highlights include blenders as anti-Russian defenses, Automotive as the last safe aisle in America, and an employee instructed to tell curious people “everything’s fine” without being told what to do if she’s the one asking.

No original emergency located. No one owns the incident. I deliver the bad news to three departments: “Code 6” does not require police. The negligent discharge absolutely did, so that part became our problem whether anyone liked it or not. I file forms no one will read and write a memo everyone will hate. My partner practices the ancient art of silent existence, which, to be fair, sometimes keeps me from snapping. The grape thief escapes justice, which we do not applaud, but I note he alone achieved a clean objective: he wanted grapes; he got grapes; he left calm.

Unresolved anomalies: towels apparently eat bullets; candles allegedly spell words. I can’t prove either. If that’s real, next week I’ll cite a unicorn for speeding.

Administration chimes in: Captain Crampton reprimands me for tone, unicorns, and consumer product reviews. He’s right that a missed lunch is not a public safety hazard; it just feels like one.

SuperMart corporate issues a statement reframing the chaos as an “unplanned customer engagement experience.” They deny bulletproof towels, then launch Miracle Absorbency Limited Edition at 60% off, inspired by “guest feedback.” Official word: “Code 6” means “Everything’s Fine.” Which is convenient, because if everyone believes it, maybe we can all pretend it is. Until the next Code 6.
